_comment: D188 Group 4 bounded Confederate finance toolkit. War Effort teaching/system only: no new playable battle, no tactical OOB rewrite, and no battle-result gate. Default bridge input is exact zero; optional finance priorities are capped, costly, and Confederate-side only.

profile.name: Confederate finance under stress
profile.summary: The toolkit turns the Confederacy's war-finance choices into visible levers: foreign cotton credit, cotton bonds, the produce loan, impressment, and the printing spiral.
profile.teaching: Confederate finance was not a missing magic key. It was a weak fiscal state trying to turn cotton, slave-capital wealth, coercive procurement, and paper promises into armies while blockade, battlefield losses, and civilian distrust ate the collateral.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md csa-finance subsystem, citing Douglas B. Ball, Financial Failure and Confederate Defeat, and Roger L. Ransom, EH.Net, The Economics of the Civil War.
data/economy.json finance.levers for Confederate bonds, taxation, foreignLoan, printing, and agriculture.impressmentLever.
data/diplomacy.json cotton-erlanger debate card and quantitative audit.

instruments.label: Erlanger cotton loan
instruments.summary: A one-time European cotton-backed loan: cash now, punitive terms, and a bond price tied to battlefield credibility and blockade-running cotton.
instruments.mechanism: The £3M face loan was taken by Erlanger at 77, offered publicly at 90, carried a 7% coupon, and ultimately realized only about 45% of face after price collapse and Confederate repurchases.
instruments.playerTradeoff: Immediate foreign cash and a small supply lift; later debt burden, pledged cotton, and credit damage.
sources: data/economy.json finance.levers.foreignLoan.CS, corrected netProceedsPct 0.45 and Erlanger buy/public offer prices.
data/diplomacy.json cotton-erlanger, citing Judith Fenner Gentry, The Erlanger Loan, Weidenmier, Britannica, and the 19 March 1863 prospectus.

instruments.label: Cotton bonds
instruments.summary: Borrow against cotton collateral and future export hopes, even as blockade and self-embargo make the collateral harder to deliver.
instruments.mechanism: Cotton-backed paper could attract buyers only while Europe believed cotton could move and Confederate independence remained plausible.
instruments.playerTradeoff: Some purchasing power and a modest supply lift; more pledged cotton and weaker credit as losses mount.
sources: data/economy.json finance.levers.bonds.CS produceLoanAuthorizedUSD_millions and cotton-collateral notes.
HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md csa-fin-king-cotton-collateral and cotton-erlanger debate cards.

instruments.label: Produce loan
instruments.summary: Ask planters to subscribe cotton and produce to government credit instead of collecting hard taxes at the start of the war.
instruments.mechanism: The 1861 Produce Loan pledged agricultural wealth to Confederate bonds, raising some funds but avoiding the harder state-capacity work of taxation.
instruments.playerTradeoff: Moderate funds and less immediate inflation than printing; more debt and planter-compliance strain.
sources: data/economy.json finance.levers.bonds.CS, Produce Loan authorization and thin bond-market notes.
HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md csa-finance summary and macro-csa-finance-doomed debate card.

instruments.label: Impressment and tithe
instruments.summary: Seize food, animals, and supplies at government prices far below market rates to keep armies moving when money fails.
instruments.mechanism: The 1863 Impressment Act and tax-in-kind could feed armies short-term while alienating farmers, encouraging hoarding, and worsening urban scarcity.
instruments.playerTradeoff: The strongest immediate supply lift; morale, fatigue, civilian pressure, and political-capital costs.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md ag-impressment-tithe debate card, citing Stephanie McCurry and Douglas B. Ball.

instruments.label: Printing spiral
instruments.summary: Cover the residue with Treasury notes: quick war funds now, compounding price collapse later.
instruments.mechanism: The Confederacy financed roughly 60% of real revenue by printing, driving money-supply growth, confidence loss, and catastrophic inflation.
instruments.playerTradeoff: Emergency funds and a small supply lift; the harshest inflation, morale, and civilian-price costs.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md csa-finance summary, citing Ball 1991, Lerner 1955, and EH.Net.

debates.title: Policy failure, not a magic escape
debates.summary: Ball argues Confederate financial policy was a real cause of defeat: too little taxation, too much printing, and coercive procurement. Other historians stress blockade and material weakness. The game gives real levers, but no lever makes low state capacity disappear.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md csa-fin-cause-vs-symptom and macro-csa-finance-doomed debate cards.
Douglas B. Ball, Financial Failure and Confederate Defeat; Roger L. Ransom, EH.Net, The Economics of the Civil War.

debates.title: Cotton collateral was fragile
debates.summary: Cotton made foreign credit plausible only while buyers believed the Confederacy could deliver bales and survive. The same collateral collapsed as blockade and battlefield defeats made delivery and independence doubtful.
sources: data/diplomacy.json cotton-erlanger source and audit notes.
HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md cotton-erlanger and csa-fin-king-cotton-collateral debate cards.

debates.title: No banker scapegoat
debates.summary: The Erlanger loan should not become a conspiracy story or an antisemitic Benjamin/Erlanger scapegoat. The deeper lesson is Confederate collateral, credit, and state-capacity failure.
sources: data/diplomacy.json cotton-erlanger Lost Cause and antisemitic-conspiracy warning.
HISTORICAL-DATA-DIPLOMACY.md Erlanger audit correction and source notes.
